howItWorks.intro: This calculator uses the Widmark formula, the standard scientific method for estimating blood alcohol content (BAC).
rules: The Widmark formula: BAC = (Alcohol consumed in grams / (Body weight in grams × r)) × 100 − (0.015 × hours)
r = Widmark factor: 0.68 for men, 0.55 for women (ratio of body water)
One standard drink contains approximately 14 grams of pure alcohol
The body metabolizes alcohol at approximately 0.015% BAC per hour
howItWorks.outro: This is an estimate only and should not be used to determine if you are safe to drive. Individual BAC varies based on food intake, medications, tolerance, metabolism, and other factors. When in doubt, do not drive.

faq.question: What is the legal BAC limit for driving?
faq.answer: In all 50 US states, the legal limit is 0.08% BAC. For commercial drivers, the limit is 0.04%. Many countries have lower limits. Note that impairment begins well below the legal limit — even 0.02% affects judgment.

faq.question: What is a standard drink?
faq.answer: In the US, one standard drink contains about 14 grams (0.6 oz) of pure alcohol. This equals approximately: 12 oz of regular beer (5% ABV), 5 oz of wine (12% ABV), or 1.5 oz of distilled spirits (40% ABV/80 proof).

faq.question: How long does it take to sober up?
faq.answer: The body metabolizes alcohol at roughly 0.015% BAC per hour. After reaching 0.08% BAC, it takes about 5.5 hours to reach 0.00%. Nothing speeds up this process — not coffee, cold showers, or exercise.

faq.question: Why does body weight affect BAC?
faq.answer: Alcohol distributes through body water. Larger people have more body water, so the same amount of alcohol is more diluted, resulting in a lower BAC. This is why the same number of drinks affects a 120-lb person more than a 200-lb person.

faq.question: Why do women generally reach higher BAC than men?
faq.answer: Women typically have a higher body fat percentage and lower body water percentage than men of the same weight. Since alcohol distributes in body water (not fat), the same amount of alcohol is more concentrated in women, leading to a higher BAC.

faq.question: Does food affect BAC?
faq.answer: Yes. Eating before or while drinking slows alcohol absorption, leading to a lower peak BAC. Food doesn't prevent intoxication, but it reduces the rate at which alcohol enters the bloodstream, spreading the effect over a longer time.

sections.title: What Is the BAC Calculator?
sections.body: The BAC Calculator is a free online tool designed for athletes and fitness enthusiasts who need quick, accurate calculations in the fitness and training space. By entering your number of standard drinks, body weight, sex, you get instant results including estimated bac, status, time until sober. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting estimated bac right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In fitness and training, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by athletes and fitness enthusiasts worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to optimize your training with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your number of standard drinks and need to find the right estimated bac. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Number of Standard Drinks
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your number of standard drinks — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: body weight, sex, hours since first drink. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your estimated bac, status, time until sober. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your number of standard drinks before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both estimated bac and status — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to optimize your training.
If you're unsure about your hours since first drink, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
